evidence_entries.evidence_text: Frames at 7s, 8s, 9s, and 10s show the same female tennis player in action. She wears a white tank top, dark skirt, and white visor. In these frames she is shown holding a racket; at 7s a tennis ball is also visible in the air. No other object is clearly visible in her hands in these action frames.

The prompt refers to a single main female tennis player. The action sequence in frames 7s-10s clearly centers on one tennis player, while the later 60s-62s frames show a group of people rather than the same centered action subject.

At 60s, a girl in a white shirt is drinking from and holding a white water bottle in a later group scene. Visual comparison across the retrieved frames indicates she is not the same person as the main action-sequence player: the action-sequence player wears a white visor, white tank top, and dark skirt and has dark hair in a ponytail, while the water-bottle holder appears in a white t-shirt without a visor and has brown hair in a ponytail.

inference_steps.text: The main character is best grounded by the 7s-10s action sequence, because those frames repeatedly center one female tennis player in play, whereas the 60s-62s material is a later group scene.

In the verified main-character frames at 7s-10s, the player holds only her racket, with the tennis ball visible at 7s; no other held object is clearly shown. That means sunglasses, a towel, a wristband, and an energy bar are unsupported for the main character in the retrieved frames.

Although a water bottle is observed at 60s, the holder is not the same person as the main action-sequence player: the main player wears a white visor, white tank top, and dark skirt and has dark hair in a ponytail, while the water-bottle holder appears in a white t-shirt without a visor and has brown hair in a ponytail. So the water bottle should be rejected for the main character.

After rejecting water bottle and finding no support for wristband, sunglasses, towel, or energy bar in the main-character frames, none of the listed options is grounded as an object briefly held by the main character.

findings.message: The trace ends with 'none of the listed options is grounded' and leaves final_answer empty. For a multiple-choice question, the trace must justify one provided option or remain explicitly unresolved due to missing answer-critical evidence; a blank/non-option outcome is not a completed answer.

The evidence only characterizes selected frames around 7s-10s and 60s-62s. From that limited coverage, the trace overreaches by concluding that none of the options is the briefly held object anywhere in the video, rather than showing that the answer-critical object was checked across the relevant grounded moments.

audit_report.feedback: The trace reasonably rejects the 60s water-bottle holder as a different person, but it does not complete the multiple-choice task and overgeneralizes from limited sampled frames to 'none of the options.'
missing_information: which provided option is the other object briefly held by the main character
grounded evidence covering the answer-critical moment(s) where the main character briefly holds an object besides the tennis ball and racket

diagnostics.referent_alignment: Partially supported: the trace textually distinguishes the 7s-10s action-sequence player from the 60s water-bottle holder using clothing and hair cues.
diagnostics.temporal_alignment: Insufficient for the full question: the trace grounds only sampled intervals, not the full video span implied by 'briefly in the video.'
diagnostics.option_alignment: Failed: no unique provided option is selected or justified.
diagnostics.evidence_sufficiency: Insufficient: the supplied evidence supports absence of other visible held objects in the sampled 7s-10s action frames and rejection of the 60s water bottle as a different person, but not a definitive none-of-the-above conclusion for the whole video.

transcript_segments.text: Alex Replier calls the tennis court her happy place. The Chamberlain Park Junior tried a lot of sports growing up, but tennis stuck. It was just something about the fact that when I'm on the court, it's me and I get to make decisions. And if you know I messed up, it's me. And if I win, it's me.

Replier started playing when she was five years old and made the CP varsity as a seventh grader. In ninth grade, she became the Rebels' number one singles player. I went from just being the flattest, hardest hitter ever to someone who I can actually hit topspin now and just really, I know how to construct points better and I know how to really just set up a strategy that'll work to have me be successful on the court.

Replier is a team captain this season and tries to be a leader and mentor to younger players. She really took me under her wing. She just made sure I felt like I could talk to her about anything. And if I had any questions, I could go to her. Year after year, she got a little more mature. She started to make friends. And she's really blossomed into the leader of Temple Park tennis.

transcript_segments.text: Replier is always striving to get better and to that end she plays on the club circuit as well. I love competing in tournaments whether they're local level or national level and the club environment is just so fun and it is something I really love to do is just compete and every weekend you know we're traveling to different cities and spending a lot of time on the court.

Still, Replier says the club tennis world is very different from high school and can sometimes be difficult to manage. I've kind of built a community with my family and with my coaches where, you know, I have a supportive group around me, but definitely when you're on the court, you're just kind of sitting on the bench yourself. It definitely can get a little, feel a little lonely. When she's not playing tennis or keeping up with her schoolwork, Alex Replier is probably teaching the game. She volunteers at Inner City Tennis in Minneapolis.

transcript_segments.text: It's the greatest feeling. Just seeing these kids kind of latch on to a sport that I love so much and just the smiles on their face when they kind of, you know, hit a forehand for the first time or, you know, something just clicks in their head where they're like, oh my god, I know how to do this. It's just a really amazing feeling to know that I have an impact on their lives and that I get to show them something that I love so much and see them love it just as much.

Champlin Park bowed out early in the section tournament, but with a pretty young squad, Replier believes the future is bright for Rebels tennis. All of these girls work so hard in the offseason, and we all play constantly almost every day in the offseason. And so as we look into the future, we know that even if this year isn't perfect, we have time to grow, and we can do even better next year and just keep working hard. Jason Melillo, CCX Sports.
Find more prep sports games and highlights at ccxmedia.org and follow us on social media.

transcript_segments.text: Replier is always striving to get better and to that end she plays on the club circuit as well. I love competing in tournaments whether they're local level or national level and the club environment is just so fun and it is something I really love to do is just compete and every weekend you know we're traveling to different cities and spending a lot of time on the court.

Still, Replier says the club tennis world is very different from high school and can sometimes be difficult to manage. I've kind of built a community with my family and with my coaches where, you know, I have a supportive group around me, but definitely when you're on the court, you're just kind of sitting on the bench yourself. It definitely can get a little, feel a little lonely. When she's not playing tennis or keeping up with her schoolwork, Alex Replier is probably teaching the game. She volunteers at Inner City Tennis in Minneapolis.

transcript_segments.text: It's the greatest feeling. Just seeing these kids kind of latch on to a sport that I love so much and just the smiles on their face when they kind of, you know, hit a forehand for the first time or, you know, something just clicks in their head where they're like, oh my god, I know how to do this. It's just a really amazing feeling to know that I have an impact on their lives and that I get to show them something that I love so much and see them love it just as much.

Champlin Park bowed out early in the section tournament, but with a pretty young squad, Replier believes the future is bright for Rebels tennis. All of these girls work so hard in the offseason, and we all play constantly almost every day in the offseason. And so as we look into the future, we know that even if this year isn't perfect, we have time to grow, and we can do even better next year and just keep working hard. Jason Melillo, CCX Sports.
Find more prep sports games and highlights at ccxmedia.org and follow us on social media.

segments.text: Alex Replier calls the tennis court her happy place. The Chamberlain Park Junior tried a lot of sports growing up, but tennis stuck. It was just something about the fact that when I'm on the court, it's me and I get to make decisions. And if you know I messed up, it's me. And if I win, it's me.

Replier started playing when she was five years old and made the CP varsity as a seventh grader. In ninth grade, she became the Rebels' number one singles player. I went from just being the flattest, hardest hitter ever to someone who I can actually hit topspin now and just really, I know how to construct points better and I know how to really just set up a strategy that'll work to have me be successful on the court.

Replier is a team captain this season and tries to be a leader and mentor to younger players. She really took me under her wing. She just made sure I felt like I could talk to her about anything. And if I had any questions, I could go to her. Year after year, she got a little more mature. She started to make friends. And she's really blossomed into the leader of Temple Park tennis.

Replier is always striving to get better and to that end she plays on the club circuit as well. I love competing in tournaments whether they're local level or national level and the club environment is just so fun and it is something I really love to do is just compete and every weekend you know we're traveling to different cities and spending a lot of time on the court.

Still, Replier says the club tennis world is very different from high school and can sometimes be difficult to manage. I've kind of built a community with my family and with my coaches where, you know, I have a supportive group around me, but definitely when you're on the court, you're just kind of sitting on the bench yourself. It definitely can get a little, feel a little lonely. When she's not playing tennis or keeping up with her schoolwork, Alex Replier is probably teaching the game. She volunteers at Inner City Tennis in Minneapolis.

It's the greatest feeling. Just seeing these kids kind of latch on to a sport that I love so much and just the smiles on their face when they kind of, you know, hit a forehand for the first time or, you know, something just clicks in their head where they're like, oh my god, I know how to do this. It's just a really amazing feeling to know that I have an impact on their lives and that I get to show them something that I love so much and see them love it just as much.

Champlin Park bowed out early in the section tournament, but with a pretty young squad, Replier believes the future is bright for Rebels tennis. All of these girls work so hard in the offseason, and we all play constantly almost every day in the offseason. And so as we look into the future, we know that even if this year isn't perfect, we have time to grow, and we can do even better next year and just keep working hard. Jason Melillo, CCX Sports.
Find more prep sports games and highlights at ccxmedia.org and follow us on social media.

transcript_spans.text: It's the greatest feeling. Just seeing these kids kind of latch on to a sport that I love so much and just the smiles on their face when they kind of, you know, hit a forehand for the first time or, you know, something just clicks in their head where they're like, oh my god, I know how to do this. It's just a really amazing feeling to know that I have an impact on their lives and that I get to show them something that I love so much and see them love it just as much.

Champlin Park bowed out early in the section tournament, but with a pretty young squad, Replier believes the future is bright for Rebels tennis. All of these girls work so hard in the offseason, and we all play constantly almost every day in the offseason. And so as we look into the future, we know that even if this year isn't perfect, we have time to grow, and we can do even better next year and just keep working hard. Jason Melillo, CCX Sports.
Find more prep sports games and highlights at ccxmedia.org and follow us on social media.
